item.title: My Interpretation
item.content:encoded:
I think Jack was already a member of 1659, and he just got promoted. That's why he was counting yellow cars. He was just y-class, but he just got promoted to Beta by his boss, who is an Alpha (I don't know how to Greek letters). This also explains why Steve, Jack's bo ss, knew the logistics and Rube Goldberg-ness of the whole thing.

Anyway, funny and interesting story. This just turned my opinion of Directorate K around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd missed this. You're actually wrong about the dialogue rules. :/ You made a bunch of grammar errors that are just errors, not just personal choices for effect. :P It's true that you can bend a lot of rules to get voice, with dialogue in particular ("What. The. Fuck." is perfectly fine in dialogue, for example — or internal monologue for that matter), but stuff like the very second sentence or ""Holy fuuuuuuuuck." Jack said, shaking his head. " [randomly picked out] are just errors, not acceptable variation. The vast majority of the errors are just errors, unfortunately.

Know the rules before you break 'em!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for extensive dialogue tag errors, and other miscellaneous grammatical errors. :( Often, I will just go through and fix these myself, but the opening didn't grab me enough to keep reading with the presence of so many errors.

The counting cars bit was really good, but the excuse given by the boss is such an obvious cop-out… eh, it didn't work for me.

If the author is around and wants help cleaning it up, feel free to shoot me a PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this story, there need to more about Dir. K.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You make an interesting point at the end there - it's certainly the case that we don't speak with perfect punctuation, and so many media (by which I mean written fiction, films, TV, etc…) end up with unnatural-sounding dialogue because of tradition that it actually becomes strange when more realistic speech is written (see, for example, The Big Lebowski) - although I personally think that realistic talking is always the best when possible.

I myself prefer to have totally correct punctuation within speech and use language to reflect the cadence and rhythm, but I can see how using punctuation to achieve the same effect is equally valid. To me as a reader, it breaks the flow and immersion a bit, but I realise this is just my interpretation, and that to others it could be easier.

Bottom line is it's the writer's choice, and as long as it isn't a deal breaker for the majority of the audience then the writer can be said to have made the correct choice. For me, your stylistic decisions certainly weren't enough to prevent my upvote. And I thank you for taking the time to explain your thought process to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the critique, and also the praise.

+ Show
- Hide

I appreciate your thoughts on dialogue, and would definitely be open to hearing more opinions on different dialogue styles and choices.

However, I carefully and deliberately wrote the dialogue in such a way as to have the punctuation and word choice match the flow of an actual conversation, and to subtly point out the mood of the speaker. Whether it was effective or not, I suppose, depends on the reader.

"Absolutely," he replied. "I sure will. May I return to my desk."

Even though it's technically a question, Jack is speaking so flatly that it doesn't sound like a question so much as a statement. I chose to illustrate this by using a period, rather than saying, "Jack said flatly" which I personally feel isn't as effective.

"What." Jack thought he misheard. He realized he didn't. "The. Fuck."

I could say, "'What the fuck,' Jack said slowly," or even, "Whaaaaaaaaaat theeeeeeee fuuuuuuuuuck???" but these all have a different feel from "What. The. Fuck." which I personally feel shows his confusion and disbelief more than the others. It shows his mind is working as he tries to sputter out words. Of course, I could say, "he stammered, his mind reeling as he uselessly sputtered out words." but that's lengthy and breaks flow in my opinion.

Just my thoughts. Dialogue doesn't need perfect punctuation because we rarely speak in perfect punctuation. I wouldn't mind a full thread on this somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd agree with TL333s here about the dialogue. It is really quite poor in a number of places: excessive use of full stops to indicate long pauses where an adjectival description would be better (e.g. "Uh, so?" said Jack slowly.), lots of clumsy verbal stalls, bad punctuation (missing commas being the worst offender), and quite flippant word usage. I'd recommend a re-write of the dialogue, as it would seriously improve the tone of the piece.

That said, I liked everything else. I like Directorate K, I like the characters (though the protagonist could stand to be a little less dumbfounded all the time, I heartily approve of his first impression being that his boss has flipped and needs help), and I love Rube-Goldberg chains of events.

Overall, a solid +1, but if I were you, I would work on dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have mixed feelings about this, but overall I liked it.

For starters, I knew it had to be a Directorate K tale as soon as it mentioned the bum keeping track of spinning quarters. This pleased me.

The dialogue after the two emails starts getting a little wonky, though. I was actually going to suggest cutting everything after "You have to be shitting me", to just leave this on a punchline, but then you'd lose the fun explanation of everything that's been going on. I like the idea that the Directorate's activities, at least on the individual level, actually make sense. There's nothing anomalous about what any of those people did; the anomaly is, again, the organization itself, that all this sort of thing can be kept track of and directed.

So like I said, I think you've hit on something really great here. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, everyone, for the praise and feedback. As my first tale (only two skips before this) I'm delighted by the positive reception.

To be honest, even in my mind, this scenario isn't par for the course as to how Directorate K normally operates. I don't have a 100% head-canon for it, yet, but I imagine that many members, including almost all the γ-class, are either memeticaly influenced, possibly from birth, or are subtly contacted at a younger age than this. I imagine the γ-class being guys in yellow ponchos, the sign-cleaners, the quarter-spinner, etc.

Steve Howitt here is either β-class or α-class, and Jack is being recruited to fill a low-level opening in one of those classes as well.

Also, this is probably one of their more overt operations. I imagine that most of the butterfly-effect influences by Directorate-K are much subtler than what's outlined in this story. But I needed to keep it somewhat short, and I needed the reader to be able to follow it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just made me realize how much potential there is in Directorate K.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's some subtle differences of interpretation I have with how Directorate K operates, but overall I enjoyed this. The dumbfounded protagonist made me chuckle several times. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. It's a great interpretation, it ties in things from the article, and I like the characters. Would +1 again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I'd believe it far more happily if you told me that they had no individual idea of what they were doing, but okay. Upvoted because it's Wacky Lobby. Wacky Lobby gets my upvote every time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh… This seems too organized for what I've interpreted from Dir. K.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This tale is one man's encounter with SCP-1659.

Thanks to Kalinin for letting me write a tale about his source material, SCP-1659. Also thanks to Kalinin for reading my initial drafts and making suggestions. Finally, I would also like to thank Kalinin for proofreading my final draft and helping me with final edits.
